paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: I just re-read what I feel is the best biography of Rockefeller: John D. The Founding Fathers of the Rockefellers by David Freeman Hawk, published in 1980. This book has the most concise description of how he actually built Standard Oil. I stripped away all biographical details and made a list of about 100 different ideas that Rockefeller used to build what Charlie Munger called the greatest company ever created.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: To Rockefeller, business resembled a form of war. He would transmit all messages in code and cover his operations with secrecy. He admitted, 'It is all too true, but I wonder what general ever sends out a brass band in advance with orders to notify the enemy that on a certain day he will begin an attack.' His father told him, 'Never mind the crowd. Keep away from it. Tend to your own business.' Rockefeller said, 'Do not many of us who fail to achieve big things fail because we lack concentration. The art of concentrating the mind on the thing to be done at the proper time and to the exclusion of everything else.'

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: From the very beginning, Rockefeller was obsessed with numbers. He would inspect every line of every bill and admonish others who failed to do so. He had extreme self-confidence from a young age. When Cornelius Vanderbilt, the richest person in America who was 45 years older, wanted to meet him, Rockefeller would only meet if Vanderbilt came to Cleveland to his office. He said, 'By the time I was a man, long before it, I had learned the underlying principles of business as well as many men acquire them by the time they are 40.'

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: When Rockefeller needed to find his first job as a teenager in a city where he knew nobody, he drew up a list of suitable firms from the city directory. Each morning he started canvassing—banks, railroads, commission houses, shipping firms. He would ask to see the man in charge. He did this six days a week, week after week. After visiting every important firm, he began the rounds again. He had relentless persistence that was slow, methodical, and just wore you down.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Rockefeller learned early that all is not how it seems on the outside. Working with transportation, he saw that posted rates that were fixed were not actually fixed—they could all be negotiated. A favored shipper would receive a rebate on the public rate that could amount to a substantial sum. That's exactly what he does when shipping oil with railroads. One of the most important things Rockefeller got good at was borrowing money. His partner called him 'the greatest borrower I ever saw.' He was a familiar face at all Cleveland banks. He said, 'What if the president of a bank refused to make me a loan? That was nothing. I must look elsewhere until I got what I wanted.'

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: When Rockefeller got into oil refining, he noticed transportation was the largest expense. It cost more to ship a barrel of oil than to refine it. He selected a refinery location next to both railroad and river, so he could ship by rail or boat. Shipping by water was 50% cheaper. From the beginning, Rockefeller was obsessed with control. He opened a workshop in the refinery and started making his own barrels so he didn't have to rely on other people. He kept borrowing like hell to get big fast.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Rockefeller forced a breakup from small-minded partners who didn't share his ambition. He did everything in secret. There's a story where he was stuck with bullying partners who controlled the votes. Quietly, he lined up financing and waited. When they tried to force him out, he called their bluff and said they should auction the firm's assets. They were sure they'd win because Rockefeller didn't have that kind of money. But Rockefeller won the auction. A few weeks later, newspapers announced his new partnership, revealing who had backed his bid. At 25 years old, Rockefeller owned one of the largest refineries in the world. He said, 'My mind had not been idle while they were talking so big and loud. How fortunate that I bought out all those men. I point to that day as the beginning of the success that I have made in my life.'

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Rockefeller was quietly charismatic. He could charm birds out of a tree. He trapped the prey before it knew the seduction had begun. He cultivated an unaggressive exterior—patient, gracious, never said an unkind word. This was a mask. Behind that placid exterior lay traits he tried hard to hide.
